Generative AI statement

# Type B: Generative AI is permitted as an assistive tool within the assessment.
# Describe how you used as AI here
# Write this as a code comment as I have done here so that this will not be included
# in the word count. Do not use code comments elsewhere as a means to include 
# additional content.

Setup

library(ggplot2)
Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3
polpres <- read.csv("polpres.csv")

The wordcount:

library(rmdwc)
rmdcount("cw26.Rmd")$words
[1] 1133

1. Additional Voters

candidates <- c("Bartoszewicz","Biejat","Braun","Hołownia","Jakubiak",
"Maciak","Mentzen","Nawrocki","Senyszyn","Stanowski",
"Trzaskowski","Woch","Zandberg")

polpres$round1 <- rowSums(polpres[, candidates])
polpres$round2 <- polpres$N2 + polpres$T2 + polpres$spoilt
polpres$additional <- polpres$round2 - polpres$round1

pp <- polpres[polpres$province != "", ]

From initial dataset analysis, the two counties ‘zagranica’ and ‘statki’ have no listed province. From exploring this further, these both translate to ‘foreign countries’ and ‘ships’. In light of this, I have excluded these two from the dataset for this question as the focus on counties implies this is a reasonable adjustment to make. Additional voters were calculated by taking the difference of voters across all 13 candidates, and the number of spoilt, ‘Nawrocki’ and ‘Trzaskowski’ votes from round 2.

lmod_before <- lm(additional ~ round1, data = pp)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) 
plot(lmod_before)

Initially, a linear model of ‘round1’ against ‘additional’ was fitted, which found round1 to be a statistically significant predictor variable for additional voters. However, after checking diagnostics plots it became obvious quickly that a linear model was not valid here. There are clear signs of heteroscedasticity, with larger counties having much higher variation. Additionally, the residuals v leverage plot reveals that observation 179 has an extremely high leverage which suggests it disproportionately influences the model fit.

lmod_before <- lm(additional ~ round1, data = pp)
lmod_after <- lm(log(additional) ~ log(round1), data = pp)

stud <- rstudent(lmod_after) 
pp$stud <- stud

n <- nrow(pp) 
threshold <- qt(0.05 / (2 * n), df = n - 2 - 1)
pp$outlier <- abs(pp$stud) > abs(threshold)

ggplot(pp, aes(log(round1), log(additional))) + geom_point() +
geom_smooth() + geom_text(data = pp[pp$outlier, ], # method = lm in here???
aes(label = county), hjust = -0.1, size = 3) + xlab("Log first round total votes") + ylab("Log additional voters in second round")
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

A log transformation to both ‘additional’ and ‘round1’ voters before fitting a linear model, as it gives more interpretable coefficients when compared to sqrt. Inspecting this models diagnostics plots, no major concerns were seen and thus a linear model appears to be a good fit. The model achieves an R^2 of 0.8276, with log(round1) a statistically significant predictor with an estimated coefficient of 0.89; implying that additional voters scales approximately proportionally with county size.

To identify outliers, studentized residuals were tested against a Bonferroni-corrected threshold, which produced three outliers: gryficki (4.45), kołobrzeski (4.43), and kamieński (4.09). All 3 of these counties are found in the zachodniopomorskie province as well as being coastal towns, suggesting inflated voter counts reflect holidaymakers voting there rather than at home.

2. Exit Poll

p <- sum(polpres$T2) / (sum(polpres$T2) + sum(polpres$N2))

Spoilt votes were excluded as these are unlikely to represent voters who truthfully revealed their choice, and we cannot make any conclusions yet about where these votes would have ended up.

Modelling the survey as 1000 independent draws from the voting population, each voter’s choice follows a Bernoulli trial, giving a Binomial(1000, p) model where p is Trzaskowski’s national vote share (excluding spoilt ballots). The proportion of votes for ‘Trzaskowski’ across the second round of voting was found to be 0.49, only marginally smaller than that of the eventual winner ‘Nawrocki’.

prob <- 1 - pbinom(500, 1000, p)

Using the binomial model, If we suppose 1000 randomly selected voters were surveyed, the probability that a majority favour Trzaskowski is 0.28. This means there is roughly a 1 in 4 chance a random sample of 1000 voters would show a Trzaskowski majority, which is plausible given his true share sits just below 50%. This means that the exit poll result wasn’t unlikely - it could easily happen by chance even when the true share is below 50%.

3. Turnout

pp <- polpres[!is.na(polpres$province) & polpres$province != "", ]
pp$turnout <- (pp$N2 + pp$T2 + pp$spoilt) / pp$eligible2 * 100

‘Zagranica’ and ‘statki’ were excluded here as they do not represent voting in Poland. To find the turnout figures for round 2, I calculated (N2 + T2 + spoilt) / eligible2 × 100 for each county, which gives a standardised measure of each county’s voting proportion.

lmod3 <- lm(turnout ~ province, data = pp)
anova(lmod3)

One-way ANOVA was used to test whether mean turnout differs across provinces. Diagnostic plots were checked and the residual-fitted and residuals-leverage plots showed no concerns. The Q-Q plot showed minor departure from normality in the lower tail, but with approximately 24 counties per province the Central Limit Theorem ensures the F-test remains reliable. The ANOVA showed highly significant differences in turnout between provinces (F(15, 364) = 14.37, p < 0.001).

ggplot(pp, aes(x = reorder(province, turnout, FUN = median), y = turnout)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip() +
  xlab("Province") +
  ylab("Turnout (%)")

The F-test conclusion is supported by the boxplot, which shows a mixed spread of province turnouts and a high degree of within-province variabilty. ‘Opolskie’ and ‘warmińsko-mazurskie’ clearly separate at the lower end while the remaining provinces form a more gradual rise in turnout. At the upper end, no province appears exceptionally high or distinct from the general trend, with ‘mazowieckie’ having the highest turnout percentage, a likely ranking given it contains ‘Warsaw’, the county with the highest official turnout seen in the dataset.

4. Spoilt Ballots

pp <- polpres[polpres$province != "", ]
pp$round1 <- rowSums(pp[, candidates])
pp$round2 <- pp$N2 + pp$T2 + pp$spoilt
pp$spoilt_rate <- pp$spoilt / pp$round2

pp$left_share <- (pp$Biejat + pp$Zandberg) / pp$round1
pp$right_share <- (pp$Braun + pp$Mentzen + pp$Nawrocki) / pp$round1
pp$centre_share <- (pp$Hołownia + pp$Trzaskowski) / pp$round1

ggplot(pp, aes(left_share, spoilt_rate)) + geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth() + xlab("Left-wing vote share") + ylab("Spoilt rate")
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

ggplot(pp, aes(right_share, spoilt_rate)) + geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth() + xlab("Right-wing vote share") + ylab("Spoilt rate")
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

ggplot(pp, aes(centre_share, spoilt_rate)) + geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth() + xlab("Centre vote share") + ylab("Spoilt rate")
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

lmod_left <- lm(spoilt_rate ~ left_share, pp)
lmod_right <- lm(spoilt_rate ~ right_share, pp)
lmod_centre <- lm(spoilt_rate ~ centre_share, pp)

After checking initial diagnostic plots, statki was excluded due to its clear outlier behavior. Spoilt rate was modelled against Trzaskowski’s first-round vote share. From the model summary we see a significant positive coefficient, meaning that counties with stronger first-round Trzaskowski support had higher spoilt rates. This suggests spoilt ballot voters were more likely to be potential Trzaskowski supporters.

5. USA-style election

The electoral college is an indirect voting system where each state is allocated a fixed number of ‘electors’, which is awarded to the candidate securing the plurality of that given state. The presidency is then determined by the candidate who achieves the majority total of electoral votes, rather than necessarily the national popular vote.

pp <- polpres[!is.na(polpres$province) & polpres$province != "", ]

prov <- aggregate(cbind(T2, N2, eligible2) ~ province, data = pp, FUN= sum) 
prov$winner <- ifelse(prov$T2 > prov$N2, "Trzaskowski", "Nawrocki")
prov$ec_votes <- prov$eligible2

Given this question concerns provinces, ‘Zagranica’ and ‘statki’ were excluded as they are not true polish counties and thus do not fall into any given province. For each province, we calculate the winning candidate who claims all electoral votes for that region. We represent each province’s electors by their number of eligible voters, mimicking the traditional, proportional representation assigned to states.

prov$margin <- abs(prov$T2 - prov$N2) 

ec_T <- sum(prov$ec_votes[prov$winner == "Trzaskowski"]) 
ec_N <- sum(prov$ec_votes[prov$winner == "Nawrocki"]) 
ec_winner <- ifelse(ec_T > ec_N, "Trzaskowski", "Nawrocki") 

ec_winner_provs <- prov[prov$winner == ec_winner, ]
ec_winner_provs <- ec_winner_provs[order(ec_winner_provs$margin), ]

ec_lead <- abs(ec_T - ec_N) 
best <- Inf
best_combo <- NULL
for (k in 1:nrow(ec_winner_provs)) {
  combos <- combn(1:nrow(ec_winner_provs), k, simplify = FALSE)
  for (combo in combos) {
    ec_swing <- sum(ec_winner_provs$ec_votes[combo]) * 2
    if (ec_swing > ec_lead) {
      cost <- sum(floor(ec_winner_provs$margin[combo] / 2) + 1)
      if (cost < best) {
        best <- cost
        best_combo <- combo
      }
    }
  }
}

Under the revised election system, only 20,333 voter changes would be needed to reverse the election outcome. Despite the high vote margin between candidates, a select set of provinces provide the necessary proportionate change to swing this result from ‘Trzaskowski’ to ‘Nawrocki’.

national_T <- sum(polpres$T2)
national_N <- sum(polpres$N2) 
national_margin <- abs(national_T - national_N) 
national_flips <- floor(national_margin / 2) + 1

The actual number of votes needed to alter the outcome of the election under the Polish electoral system is 184,796, which represents the raw difference in candidate votes between the two options of round 2. Clearly, this number is almost 10 times larger than that of the electoral college system. This is due to the electoral system’s sensitivity to small vote changes and marginal shifts in regions shaping national outcomes. This contrasts Poland’s direct popular vote system, which reflects overall opinions and voting changes more proportionally.

6. Transfer of first round votes

pp <- polpres
pp$round1 <- rowSums(pp[, candidates])
pp$round2 <- pp$N2 + pp$T2 + pp$spoilt
pp$others <- pp$Bartoszewicz + pp$Jakubiak + pp$Maciak + 
             pp$Senyszyn + pp$Stanowski + pp$Woch
pp$latejoins <- pp$round2 - pp$round1

lmod6 <- lm(T2 ~ Biejat + Zandberg + Hołownia + Braun + Mentzen + 
             Trzaskowski + Nawrocki + others + latejoins - 1, data = pp)
summary(lmod6)

Call:
lm(formula = T2 ~ Biejat + Zandberg + Hołownia + Braun + Mentzen + 
    Trzaskowski + Nawrocki + others + latejoins - 1, data = pp)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1174.74  -254.55   -90.21    94.54  1991.97 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
Biejat       0.862436   0.056225  15.339  < 2e-16 ***
Zandberg     1.045147   0.020382  51.278  < 2e-16 ***
Hołownia     0.633463   0.058541  10.821  < 2e-16 ***
Braun        0.278820   0.040439   6.895 2.31e-11 ***
Mentzen      0.175842   0.023585   7.456 6.31e-13 ***
Trzaskowski  1.083294   0.006568 164.934  < 2e-16 ***
Nawrocki    -0.209841   0.007620 -27.539  < 2e-16 ***
others       0.727024   0.100990   7.199 3.37e-12 ***
latejoins    0.753252   0.013784  54.648  < 2e-16 ***
---

Residual standard error: 366.8 on 373 degrees of freedom
